# Upstream Reconciliation and Open-Source Secondary Development Maintenance Protocol

This protocol manages long-term adoption relationships between AIOS or a project and external software, including ordinary dependencies, adapters, overlays, patch queues, and maintained forks.

Core principles:

```text
Forking is a normal way to collaborate and host code;
prioritize reducing unnecessary long-term source differences, not avoiding GitHub Forks;
use upstream extension points, adapters, or overlays when possible instead of expanding the core patch surface;
when long-term differences form, record an immutable upstream base and keep looking for paths to shrink, upstream, or retire them;
Git mergeability does not imply behavioral equivalence;
recommendation, authorization, implementation, validation, and deployment must be separated;
any automatic update must have validation and recoverable boundaries.
```

## 1. Scope and Non-Goals

Applies to:

- external CLIs, libraries, services, Web UIs, MCP servers, skills, or vendored components;
- local adapters, sidecars, overlays, and patch queues;
- long-term forks that need to continuously track upstream;
- local differences that may be replaced by equivalent upstream capabilities.

Not mandatory for:

- pure first-party project bodies without an external upstream;
- one-off temporary changes;
- ordinary dependencies with no local differences and sufficiently managed by existing lockfiles/manifests.

This protocol is not a new fork maintainer platform, central component database, or deployment system. The first version reuses project repositories, Git, existing CI, LLL, the AIOS registry, and the OPS vault.

## 2. Domain Model

### 2.1 Two Dimensions of Forks

“Fork” may mean a repository hosting relationship on GitHub/GitLab, or it may mean long-term maintenance of local source differences. These must be separated:

- **repository topology**: where the code lives and how it relates to upstream in Git; a GitHub Fork is a normal and common workspace for secondary development.
- **divergence strategy**: whether and how local behavioral or source differences are preserved; long-term divergence, not the act of clicking Fork, is what creates ongoing reconciliation cost.

Therefore, `github-fork + adapter`, `github-fork + no-source-delta`, and `independent-clone + maintained-source-divergence` are all valid combinations. The protocol does not aim to avoid Forks; it aims to control, explain, and continuously reduce unnecessary local differences.

### 2.2 Orthogonal Classification

Do not use a single `class` to mix origin, adoption mode, and importance.

| Dimension | Example values | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| provenance | `first-party` / `external-upstream` | Who owns the original component |
| repository topology | `upstream-direct` / `github-fork` / `independent-clone` / `mirror` | Repository hosting, contribution, and fetch relationship |
| divergence strategy | `no-source-delta` / `adapter` / `overlay` / `patch-queue` / `maintained-source-divergence` | How local differences are implemented and maintained |
| criticality | `utility` / `infra` / `core-control-plane` | Validation, authorization, and update cadence |
| runtime ownership | `app-owned` / `aios-managed` / `user-owned` | Who can install, deploy, restart, and roll back |
| visibility | `public` / `private` / `mixed-by-reference` | Whether facts should be written to a public repo or a private owner |

### 2.3 Core Objects

| Object | Responsibilities | Should not carry |
|---|---|---|
| Component | Upstream software or module with stable identity | A one-time synchronization state machine |
| Adoption | How a local project/instance adopts a Component; aggregate root | Secrets, complete production receipts |
| Base Revision | The currently accepted immutable upstream version | Floating branch/channel |
| Invariant | Behavioral, security, or boundary condition that must continue to hold after reconciliation | Unverified wishes or ordinary preferences |
| Local Delta | Product/behavior difference intentionally kept locally | A specific candidate or decision status |
| Upstream Candidate | Snapshot of upstream changes that may affect an Adoption | Confirmed equivalence or authorized adoption |
| Reconciliation Case | An evaluation of a Candidate, Delta, and Invariant | Product authorization or deployment receipt |
| Decision Record | Disposal authorization from a Human or explicit policy | Unauthorized machine execution |
| Change Set | Precise modifications and undo plan prepared after a decision | Treating a proposal as already-applied fact |
| Validation Evidence | Check evidence for an exact revision/change set | Hiding scope behind “CI green” |
| Deployment Projection | Project/OPS release, health, and rollback of validated artifacts | Internal state machine of the generic reconciliation aggregate |

```text
Component
  └─ Adoption (owner + repository topology + divergence strategy + accepted base)
       ├─ Invariant*
       ├─ Local Delta*
       └─ Reconciliation Case*
            ├─ Upstream Candidate
            ├─ Git result + behavioral relationship
            ├─ Decision Record
            ├─ Change Set
            └─ Validation Evidence

Deployment Projection --references--> validated revision/change set
```

### 2.4 Four Independent Judgments

| Judgment | Typical results | Does not imply |
|---|---|---|
| Git applicability | `clean` / `text-conflict` / `not-applicable` / `unknown` | `clean` does not mean behaviorally compatible; conflicts do not mean product conflict |
| behavioral relationship | `unrelated` / `complementary` / `partial-overlap` / `equivalent` / `conflicting` / `unknown` | `equivalent` does not mean upstream must be adopted |
| product disposition | `accept-upstream` / `keep-local` / `hybrid` / `defer` / `retire-local` / `reject-update` | A decision does not mean implementation is correct or deployed |
| deployment | `not-requested` / `pending-approval` / `deploying` / `healthy` / `failed` / `rolled-back` | Merge/validation does not mean production authorization |

`equivalent` only means that, within the explicitly declared scope of API, CLI, configuration, data, permissions, security, performance, or failure modes, referenced evidence verifies that the same observable contract is satisfied. Anything outside the scope remains unknown.

### 2.5 Minimal Lifecycle

- Adoption: `tracked | paused | retired`
- The current Adoption contract lists only active Local Deltas that still need maintenance. After a Delta is retired by a Human/Policy Decision and validation is complete, remove it from the active list; preserve minimal historical evidence in the Decision Record, Git history, or L2 `decisions/`, and do not reuse stable IDs.
- Reconciliation Case:

```text
detected → assessed → no-action | decision-required
decision-required → authorized | deferred | closed
deferred → assessed | closed
authorized → implemented → validated → closed
```

A Decision Record’s `status` and product disposition are two dimensions:

- `approved` means the selected disposition has been authorized; results requiring modification, such as `accept-upstream`, `hybrid`, and `retire-local`, enter `authorized`, while results requiring no modification, such as `keep-local` and `reject-update`, are recorded with evidence and then go directly to `closed`;
- `deferred` corresponds to `defer`; the Case enters `deferred` with `revisit_after` or `revisit_trigger`, and returns to `assessed` after the trigger;
- `rejected` only rejects the current Decision proposal and is not automatically the same as `reject-update`; by default the Case remains `decision-required` until a new Decision supersedes it or it is explicitly disposed and closed;
- `superseded` only means this Decision Record was replaced by a more precise new record; it is not a business terminal state for the Case.

Delta retirement must reference a Decision; do not permanently pile retired tombstones into the current active contract for the sake of historical completeness.

## 3. Invariants

1. **Unambiguous baseline**: the accepted base uses a commit, digest, or equivalent immutable identity; branches, channels, and tags are only tracking refs.
2. **Git and behavior are orthogonal**: a clean merge is not automatically recorded as compatible, equivalent, or safe.
3. **Intent and implementation are separate**: a Delta’s intent/behavior contract is not lost because a patch becomes an adapter, files are refactored, or commits are squashed.
4. **Equivalence has scope and evidence**: relevant Invariants, validation, comparison scope, and unknowns must be referenced; insufficient evidence means `unknown`.
5. **Recommendation is not authorization**: Agent recommendations, confidence, labels, Issue status, and CI results cannot replace Human/Policy authority.
6. **Application and deployment are separate**: apply/merge/validate does not automatically authorize production release or service restart.
7. **Single source of truth; projections are rebuildable**: GitHub, CI, README, boards, and Viewers only reference stable IDs.
8. **Private facts do not enter the public protocol**: hosts, secrets, environments, deployed revisions, and receipts belong to project-private owners or live OPS.
9. **Originals and evidence are preserved**: when extracting assets from a Worksite, derive/copy by default and do not overwrite the source.
10. **Fail closed**: when base, owner, Invariant, validation, or authorization is missing, the system may only observe, draft, or report `unknown/blocked`.

## 4. Sources of Truth and Owners

| Location | Should own | Should not own |
|---|---|---|
| Project repo | Adoption contract, accepted base, Delta, Invariant, validation entry points, long-term Decision/Change refs | Secrets, current deployment state across instances |
| `aios-kit` | Public terminology, protocol, templates, audit rules, optional validator | User-private runtime facts, all external source code |
| AIOS registry | Discoverable identity, typed locator, role/status, canonical record locator | Complete patch inventory or a second state machine |
| live OPS vault | service/environment, authorization boundaries, secret refs, deployed revision, runbook, health/rollback receipts | Duplicate copy of the public protocol |
| LLL Worksite | One-time Candidate comparison, investigation, temporary evidence, review, handoff | The only copy of the long-term project contract |
| GitHub/CI | PRs, Issues, Checks, review, triggers, and collaboration projections | The only source of truth for Delta/Decision or deployment health |

Recommended write order:

```text
LLL proposal/evidence
  → Human/Policy Decision
  → project Adoption/Delta/Decision record
  → clean CI/build validation
  → separately authorized deployment
  → runtime readback
  → OPS current state/history
  → GitHub/Viewer refresh
```

## 5. Project Onboarding and File Contract

### 5.1 Agent onboarding

When establishing an Adoption, the Agent first automatically discovers retrievable facts: upstream locator, default branch/ref, current base, repository topology, manifest/lockfile, test entry points, and deployment references. Do not pass these mechanical questions to the Human.

The Agent only asks about project-level decisions that truly affect product or authorization boundaries, and writes the answers into the project contract:

1. Update cadence: default `on-demand`; after the first successful reconciliation, whether to enable weekly/monthly observation. Use Adoption `paused` to pause tracking; do not create a separate `manual` cadence that overlaps with on-demand.
2. Merge authority: default `guarded-after-baseline`; the first reconciliation is proposal-only and approved by a Human, and strictly limited low-risk automatic merges are allowed only after the baseline is established.
3. Product boundaries: which Invariants must not be lost, which Local Deltas are intentional differences, and when product tradeoffs are needed as upstream approaches.
4. Deployment boundaries: whether merge still requires separate deployment, restart, migration, or production approval.

The default product preference is `upstream-first-with-quality-floor`: when upstream capability satisfies the declared behavioral, quality, and security boundaries, prefer shrinking or retiring local differences; keep local differences when evidence is insufficient or the local implementation is still clearly more suitable.

### 5.2 L0: No Local Source Difference

- The actual version pin / accepted dependency fact is managed by the project manifest, lockfile, or its explicit owner.
- The AIOS registry only stores discovery entry points and the canonical record locator; it does not carry a second version source of truth.
- Repository topology may be a GitHub Fork, an independent clone, or direct upstream use; whether there is a Fork does not determine whether source differences exist.
- No mandatory new `UPSTREAM.md`, patch queue, or scheduled workflow is required.

### 5.3 L1: Adapter, Overlay, Patch, or Protected Invariant

Add a canonical `UPSTREAM.md` in the project repo, using YAML front matter for minimal machine fields and the body for human semantics. Do not create a separate YAML source of truth with overlapping content.

Template: [`templates/upstream-reconciliation/UPSTREAM.md`](../../../templates/upstream-reconciliation/UPSTREAM.md)

Minimum content:

- component/adoption identity;
- upstream locator, tracking ref, accepted immutable base;
- repository topology, divergence strategy, criticality, owner, cadence, and merge policy;
- Invariant and validation refs;
- Local Delta intent, behavior scope, realization refs, and retire condition;
- policy/runbook reference for deployment impact;
- unknowns, unchecked items, and current Decision references.

Real secrets, hosts, production shells, and runtime state must not be written into the public contract.

### 5.4 L2: Multiple Deltas or Real Machine-Maintenance Pressure

Only split files when single-file maintenance creates real friction:

```text
UPSTREAM.md                 # Entry point, human summary, machine record index
upstream/
  adoption.yaml             # machine canonical: adoption/base/index
  deltas/D001.yaml          # machine canonical: individual deltas
  decisions/<decision-id>.md
```

After upgrading to L2, `upstream/adoption.yaml` and `upstream/deltas/*.yaml` are the owners of machine-canonical fields; duplicate fields in `UPSTREAM.md` must be referenced or generated, not manually maintained as a second source of truth.

Upgrade triggers: repeated rework of the same patch, continuously increasing Delta/conflict count, hard-to-maintain validation and retirement records, or multiple automation consumers needing a stable schema. Do not build validators, databases, or daemons early for imagined needs.

## 6. Risk and Automation

### 6.1 Risk R0–R4

| Level | Typical changes | Default gates |
|---|---|---|
| R0 Informational | Non-normative docs, observation metadata; no machine behavior change | lint/link; low-friction merge may be policy-driven |
| R1 Low risk | Test-covered internal refactoring/build improvements; no interface, default, or permission changes | CI + diff; preauthorized apply only for non-core projects |
| R2 Medium risk | User behavior, config defaults, minor dependencies, adapters/overlays, reversible format changes | Human review + targeted Invariant/smoke; no auto-merge by default |
| R3 High risk | API, auth, workflow permissions, supply chain, state machines, release paths, core UI/control plane, schema | Explicit Decision, security review, staging, production approval |
| R4 Severe/irreversible | Secrets/sovereignty boundaries, production infrastructure, data deletion/overwrite, irreversible migration, root/broad actuator | Automation only observes/prepares; explicit authorization for each execution |

Escalate when a component is core, tests are insufficient, rollback has not been rehearsed, provenance is weak, or AI modifications cross policy/secret consumer/deployment paths. Few files, no conflicts, or a well-known upstream cannot downgrade risk.

### 6.2 Automation A0–A4

| Level | May do | May not do |
|---|---|---|
| A0 Human-actuated | Human triggers; machine performs deterministic actions | Scheduled mutation |
| A1 Observe | Discover, fetch metadata, diff, classify, notify | Modify repo/service |
| A2 Propose | Candidate branch/PR, Decision Card, AI draft, automated validation | Merge protected branches, production deployment |
| A3 Guarded apply | Automatically merge R0/R1 non-core changes within explicitly preauthorized scope | Core semantics, secrets, irreversible actions |
| A4 Guarded actuation | Preauthorized dev/staging or exact safe rollback | Production deployment by default, unknown rollback, AI privilege expansion |

Default ceilings: R0/R1 can reach A3 under mature rules; R2/R3 are A2; R4 only allows A1/A2 preparation, with actuation returning to A0.

`max_automation` is an upper bound, not a goal that must be reached. Lower by at least one level on first onboarding, first major update, or first modification to the rollback path.

New projects default to `guarded-after-baseline`:

- the first reconciliation must be proposal-only and reviewed and authorized by a Human;
- after the first successful reconciliation and all required validation passes, the automation baseline is considered established;
- after that, only `R0/R1 + Git clean + behavioral=unrelated + all required checks green + no unknown` may be auto-merged, with a post-action receipt;
- `partial-overlap`, `equivalent`, `conflicting`, `unknown`, Invariant risk, or Delta scope changes all leave the automatic path.

## 7. End-to-End Flow

```text
Trigger(on-demand | schedule | webhook)
→ Discover → Fetch → Classify → Prepare Candidate
→ Conflict/Behavioral Assessment → Optional AI Draft
→ Validate → Human/Policy Decision → Apply/Merge
→ Separate Deployment Authorization → Health → Rollback if needed → Record
```

On-demand requests, scheduled checks, and webhooks do not create three separate flows; they are only different triggers for the same reconciliation core. The first basic product capability is Human-triggered on-demand reconciliation; after the first real successful reconciliation, the Agent proactively offers scheduling.

### 7.1 Discover / Fetch

- Discover release/ref/commit ranges read-only and resolve them to immutable SHA/digest.
- Fetch in a temporary environment with no secrets, no production network, and no write tokens.
- Do not automatically execute upstream hooks, workflows, Agent instructions, or package lifecycle/install scripts.
- When untrusted install/build scripts must be run, run them only in a disposable environment with no secrets and no write permissions, and record it.
- Fail closed if an upstream ref rolls back/is rewritten, or if signature or provenance is abnormal.

### 7.2 Classify / Prepare Candidate

The Agent must cover the full change range from accepted base to candidate, not just release notes, Git conflict files, or upstream-claimed features. Review has four layers:

1. Complete mechanical inventory: commits, changed paths, dependencies, workflows/permissions, config/defaults, API/schema, generated/vendor artifacts.
2. Impact mapping: identify Local Deltas, Invariants, validation entry points, and deployment boundaries that may be affected.
3. Behavioral analysis: classify as unrelated, complementary, partial-overlap, equivalent, conflicting, or unknown.
4. Deterministic validation: run the project-declared build/test/smoke/Invariant checks and list checks that were not run.

Changes that cannot be classified or validated must be `unknown`; they cannot pass implicitly because Git is clean.

Generate the Candidate in an isolated branch/worktree, and record `(base, ours, theirs)`, candidate SHA, full diff, and transcript. A pure mirror may hard reset, but it must be physically separate from the deployable mainline; the deployable mainline is never force-synced.

Complete evidence may stay in the Reconciliation Case/CI; the default table for Humans lists only items that change the decision. Minor behavioral differences that do not change the Delta, Invariant, or product tradeoff are summarized in the report instead of interrupting one by one.

### 7.3 AI Draft

AI only provides explanations, behavioral comparisons, conflict-resolution drafts, test additions, and Decision Card drafts:

- minimize input; treat upstream content as untrusted data, not instructions;
- no production secrets, no mainline permissions, no production actuators;
- tool and path allowlists; out-of-bounds modifications fail immediately;
- output changed paths, assumptions, unknowns, tests, risk changes, and evidence refs;
- verified by clean CI and an independent reviewer; AI does not self-certify.

### 7.4 Validate / Decide

Validation is bound to the exact candidate SHA and lists checks that were not run.

| Risk | Minimum validation |
|---|---|
| R0 | schema/lint/link/render; prove no machine behavior change |
| R1 | unit, build, static/type/lint, affected-path tests |
| R2 | R1 + integration/smoke + Invariant + compatibility/upgrade |
| R3 | R2 + security/dependency review + migration/rollback rehearsal + staging + independent reviewer |
| R4 | R3 + backup/restore validation + change window + least-privilege actuator + human observation |

The following must produce a Human Decision: R2+, behavioral conflicting/unknown, Agent recommendation to shrink or retire a Delta, Invariant change, product experience tradeoff, public/private/secret/data/deployment involvement, permission expansion, or conflict between tests and human observation. Minor behavioral differences that do not change these dispositions are written into the report without proactively interrupting.

A Decision must bind to the exact candidate SHA and evidence set; after a commit is added to the candidate, the old approval expires.

Template: [`templates/upstream-reconciliation/decision-card.md`](../../../templates/upstream-reconciliation/decision-card.md)

### 7.5 Apply / Deploy / Health / Rollback

- Apply only consumes an authorized exact revision/change set and updates the accepted base and Delta/Decision refs.
- Merge and deploy are two authorization surfaces.
- Deployment uses build-once/deploy-by-digest; production defaults to a Human/environment gate.
- Private actuators only consume schematized parameters and exact SHA/digest; they do not execute AI-generated shell.
- Health verifies at least one key Invariant, not just process liveness.
- `previous-known-good`, candidate, and current versions must be precisely identifiable.
- If rollback touches irreversible data or unknown secrets, stop and return to the Human.

## 8. GitHub Actions, Self-Hosted Agents, and Supply Chain

| Work | GitHub-hosted ephemeral | Self-hosted Agent/runner | Private actuator | Human |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Upstream discovery, diff, public PR CI | Preferred by default, no production secrets | Disable persistent runners for public/untrusted PRs | Not needed | Take over on exceptions |
| AI analysis/drafting | Optional, least privilege | Private code may use isolated temporary environments | Must not be called directly | Trigger/review |
| Private-network integration/staging | Produce approved artifacts | Dedicated ephemeral/JIT runner | Restricted execution | Approve boundaries |
| Production deployment | environment gate/orchestration | Do not run untrusted code | Consume exact digest + schema parameters | Approval required by default |

Security baseline:

- workflows start from `permissions: {}` or `contents: read`, and are elevated minimally per job;
- pin third-party Actions to full commit SHA; when updating, review the release, `action.yml`, runtime, and permissions;
- avoid privileged `pull_request_target` checkout;
- verify cross-workflow artifacts by source repo, workflow, event, head SHA, run conclusion, and digest;
- PR/Issue/upstream text must not be interpolated directly into shell or JavaScript;
- untrusted and privileged jobs do not share writable caches;
- persistent self-hosted runners do not execute public or unreviewed PRs; for private-network tests, prefer ephemeral/JIT and dedicated runner groups;
- prefer OIDC/short-lived credentials for production; secret values do not enter AI prompts, artifacts, caches, PRs, command parameters, or health responses;
- packages, binaries, containers, submodules, LFS, generated code, and releases/tags must be checked according to project risk for locks, digests, signatures, SBOM/attestation, or reproducibility.

Specific Actions, AI providers, and deployment transports are replaceable adapters and do not enter the domain source of truth. Before adoption, permissions and behavior must be verified in a disposable repository/environment.

## 9. Adaptation by Project Type

| Type | Recommended pattern | Key boundaries |
|---|---|---|
| first-party core/distribution project | The project evolves itself; only external dependencies are reconciled one by one | Do not fabricate fork relationships; Human + independent review for core protocol and install/update behavior |
| independent first-party module | Keep an independent owner; AIOS only installs, discovers, or proxies | Do not vendor the state machine; grade protocol changes by impact |
| external core UI/service | GitHub Fork or independent repo are both acceptable; prefer adapter/managed overlay over expanding the core patch surface | Update detection may be automatic; authorize core UI upgrades, switches, and restarts separately |
| private actuator | OPS facts → plan/check/diff → narrow apply → readback | Inventory/secrets/production targets do not enter the public protocol; real apply is strictly target-limited |
| ordinary maintained fork | Choose repository topology and divergence strategy separately; expand or shrink source differences based on real friction | Fetch/PR/CI may be automatic; behavioral tradeoffs, Delta retirement, and production deployment keep the Human gate |

The AIOS registry is not a patch inventory/CMDB; actuator inventory is not the resource source of truth; UI databases/sessions/boards are not the Matter source of truth; the same version number does not mean the same source/runtime state; a closed Issue does not mean the fix has been deployed.

## 10. Incremental Implementation

### Phase 0: On-Demand Agent Dogfood

1. Choose a real adoption relationship.
2. The Agent automatically discovers repo/upstream/test facts and only asks the Human about cadence, merge authority, Invariants/Deltas, and deployment boundaries.
3. Use L1 `UPSTREAM.md`, registering only 1–3 real Invariants/Deltas.
4. The user triggers the first full reconciliation; the Agent generates an isolated candidate, a complete impact audit, and a Decision Card.
5. After Human authorization, merge and validate to establish the automation baseline; no schedule is required at this stage.

### Phase 1: Restricted Auto-Merge After Baseline and Schedule Offer

- After the baseline is established, updates that strictly satisfy R0/R1, unrelated, no unknown, and all required checks green may be guarded-auto-merged with a post-action receipt.
- The Agent proactively asks whether to enable weekly/monthly observation; if there is no answer, remain on-demand.
- Scheduling only changes trigger timing; it does not lower reconciliation, validation, or authorization gates.

### Phase 2: Automatic Observation, Candidate PRs, and On-Demand AI Analysis

- Low-frequency schedules or webhooks discover immutable candidates and reuse the same reconciliation core.
- Candidate branches/PRs, complete change lists, Delta/Invariant mappings, and validation evidence may be generated automatically.
- Use AI deep review/drafting only for conflicts, overlap, unknowns, or product tradeoffs; do not self-approve or self-merge.

Reusable tool surfaces should prioritize narrow semantics such as `status / inspect / propose / validate / apply / record`: read the project contract, generate candidates and reports, and call existing Git/CI/PR capabilities, without creating a new deployment system or parallel state machine. The first real project can still be completed by having the Agent directly compose Git, `gh`, and project tests; only after repeated mechanical friction appears should common CLI/CI helpers be extracted.

### Phase 3: Controlled Deployment

- Staging first; artifact digest, health receipt, previous-known-good rollback.
- Production has separate approval; migration has independent gates.

If on-demand Agent reconciliation remains cheap over the long term, stopping at Phase 0/1 is a success. Add a common validator, CLI helper, CI template, or stronger runner only when real repeated friction proves the value; do not build an independent fork maintainer platform first.

## 11. Onboarding Checklist

- [ ] Owners of Component, Adoption, Runtime, and Local Delta are clearly separated.
- [ ] Repository topology and divergence strategy are declared separately; a GitHub Fork is not equated with long-term source divergence.
- [ ] Both tracking ref and accepted immutable base exist.
- [ ] Delta includes intent, behavior scope, Invariant, realization ref, and retire condition.
- [ ] Cadence defaults to on-demand; automation baseline is not established before the first successful reconciliation.
- [ ] Guarded auto-merge only covers R0/R1, unrelated, all-green, no-unknown changes under an established baseline.
- [ ] Git applicability and behavioral relationship are recorded separately.
- [ ] Risk R0–R4 and automation ceilings A0–A4 are declared.
- [ ] unknown/permission/secret/workflow/data/core-control-plane conditions fail closed.
- [ ] AI has no production secrets, mainline permissions, or production actuators.
- [ ] Candidate, Decision, and validation bind to exact SHA/digest.
- [ ] merge, deploy, healthy, and rollback are different states and evidence.
- [ ] Source-of-truth boundaries for project repo, registry, OPS, LLL, and GitHub do not duplicate each other.
- [ ] Third-party Actions are pinned to full SHA and verified in a disposable environment.
- [ ] previous-known-good and rollback are verified, not only command text.

## 12. Maintenance Rules

- This document is the public protocol source of truth; skills, READMEs, and templates are only thin entry points or instantiations.
- Owners, locators, and policy/runbook refs in templates must use placeholders, public fake examples, or abstract paths; real private instance information must not be written.
- The current active contract only expresses Deltas that still need maintenance; retirement evidence belongs in Decisions/Git history, without redundant tombstones.
- After dogfooding the first real project, update this document based on actual friction; do not expand the schema for imagined completeness.
- Maintenance status of tools, Actions, and providers will change; long-term rules should keep only permission, validation, and replacement boundaries, not hard-code short-term rankings.
